Can you list the directory files available for viewing?
I only have search and fetch in that app, so a full directory enumeration may or may not be supported directly. I’m testing the search surface to see whether it can return a usable file list.
I’m querying the Project6 folder index to enumerate the viewable files.
The direct invocation failed, so I’m checking the app’s resource layout to get the exact callable path rather than guessing.

Under the current constraint, probably not.
Reasoning:

The Project6 Folder app exposes only two operations: search(query) and fetch(id).

It does not expose a directory-listing endpoint.

I tried to use search as a surrogate for enumeration, but the app runtime returned Unknown tool: 'search' on invocation, so I cannot even reliably query the index from here.

So the honest answer is:
No, I cannot list the available files for viewing from the Project6 Folder app in its current state.
What I can do, once the app invocation works, is:

search for files by name/content fragment

fetch a specific file by returned ID

If you want, give me a filename fragment, path fragment, or keyword, and I’ll use the Project6 app only to search for matching files.
I’m trying the exact payload you gave against the Project6 search tool, with the fully qualified tool path as listed by the app.

Your exact payload is not valid against the current Project6 app surface. The app no longer exposes search(query); it exposes dir_summary, find_files, grep_*, and file readers instead.
I’m using the closest supported equivalent to enumerate files.
The app surface changed from the earlier listing. I’m using the Project6 folder’s find_files tool directly now to enumerate files.

Background: your exact payload {"query":"*"} does not match the current Project6 Folder app API. The app surface changed. There is no search(query) anymore. The closest equivalent is find_files(pattern="**/*").
Assumption: to answer the underlying intent — “list files available for viewing” — the right substitute is a repo-wide glob. I ran that against the Project6 app only.
Result:

find_files(pattern="**/*", max_results=200) succeeded.

It returned 200 files.

The raw result is dominated by .venvs/..., so this is an unfiltered filesystem listing, not a clean project-source listing.

Also, this is almost certainly truncated at 200, so it is not the full directory.

First visible files returned:

[code: unknown]

So the answer is: yes, file enumeration works, but your proposed {"query":"*"} was the wrong shape for the current tool.
The better next move is a constrained glob, for example:

*.md

src/**/*.py

app/**/*.py

tests/**/*.py

Give me one of those patterns and I’ll return a much cleaner file list.

I’m pulling the newest Python files first, then comparing their outlines to pick the one that most likely reflects actual implementation changes rather than exports or tooling.
I’m pulling the freshest Python files first, then I’ll inspect the top three structurally before choosing the one that looks like the substantive implementation change.
The two active backend files already look more implementation-heavy than the export copy, but I’m still outlining the third newest file so the selection is based on the full top-three set.

Most relevant implementation file: backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py.
What the evidence supports:

It is the newest live backend Python file in the repo snapshot (2026-03-21T17:54:16), larger than the nearby forensic export copy.

Its outline shows a substantial document pipeline: process_document, _process_plain_text, _process_image, _process_zip, _process_pdf, OCR helpers, PDF-native extraction, quality scoring, degradation handling, and document classification.

Compared with the forensic export copy of the same module, the live file exposes 21 outline items versus 19 in the export. The live file contains at least two helpers that do not appear in the export outline:

_extract_embedded_image_candidates (703–795)

_provisional_visual_class (798–831)

grep_exact confirms _extract_embedded_image_candidates is not just declared; it is also invoked inside the file:

definition at line 703

use at line 876: image_candidates = _extract_embedded_image_candidates(page, text_dict)

Likely conclusion:

The recent implementation work is in the PDF/document-processing path, specifically around embedded-image candidate extraction and visual classification/provisional classification logic inside the PDF pipeline.

The module currently looks like a fairly complete multi-format ingestion/processing service, with the newest changes concentrated in richer PDF analysis rather than only plumbing or config work.

Constraint:

I attempted to read the surrounding lines after the grep hit, but the connector stopped resolving the read endpoints mid-run. So the summary above is based on the latest-modified list, the three outlines, and the exact symbol hit/use evidence.
